item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: That's good enough for us, so I've restored it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I took the photo myself, at a park in salem oregon. I think it was a riverfront somewhere.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image used in this article could not be sourced, and thus it is to be assumed that it is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

EDIT: The image has been restored with confirmation that it was taken by the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't say I don't agree! It definitely added a certain additional charm to the concept.

Maybe someday, if the definition of the class is ever expanded

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, that's a shame. Would've been fun to have a Thaumiel that, unlike others, is almost comedically unimpressive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I've done some fairly heavy edits based on critical feedback. I think this version is significantly better in a few respects!

Some minor tweaks were also made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article is well written, and I really liked the implication at the end with their slowly declining population, but I have a general distaste for articles where you can basically replace "anomalous" with "magic" and have it mean the same thing. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmhmm! In the first few drafts they were, but it was pointed out to me that thaumiel class is specifically reserved for top secret anomalies that are irreplacable in the containment of very dangerous entities

as they are neither top secret, nor irreplacable, they are safe

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering the mayflies are being used in containment of other anomalies is there a reason this isn't Thaumiel?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rather, they are sensitive to any change in their environment, just as actual mayflies are sensitive to almost any tampering with their environment, regardless of origin.

There is not just one thing they are sensitive to, that was never the concept; rather, as an indicator species, they are tied to the health of their environment, and sensitive to anything that is not part of it.

Ergo, they are not sensitive to something because it is anomalous.

They are sensitive to it because it affects them and the space they inhabit in a way that they are not prepared to compensate for, regardless of what it is.

Okay, that's certainly more reasonable. It could stand to be emphasized more in the main document, IMO, but I dislike this article significantly less than I did before.

It still leaves the question of why reality warping, telepathy, and so on aren't part of the mayflies' environment. Why are these mayflies so dependent on a static environment in a way that no other known species is?

Presumably the susceptibility to these phenomena is an acceptable tradeoff, in evolutionary terms, for the comparative ability to thrive in environments free of these phenomena. This isn't addressed in the article… although, wait. It "spread across North America in just the last two decades". I'm having trouble finding data, but you wouldn't expect such a sensitive species to find purchase all around, right? That could stand to be highlighted as well, perhaps — maybe consult with a biologist.

Maybe if it had some minor anomalous property that gives it a survival advantage under regular conditions, but fucks them up when things change, that would be more plausible. But don't do that just to please me, consider how it'll affect the article.

item.title: U
item.content:encoded:
Honestly the properties and behavior towards an anomalous items or anomalous area, just makes it soooo good (In my opinion).

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it possible, in-universe, that there's some force of nature that underlies all of these phenomena and makes this single species of mayfly die? Yeah, but I wouldn't bet my pocket lint on it, much less money.

I'm the total opposite- I sort of take it for granted that there is a "grand unified anomaly theory" most anomalies are rooted in somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Indeed, it is certainly a matter of taste. And I cannot argue against the notion that this is a well-written piece even if I don't agree with the execution. Best of luck to you in your future writing endeavours on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've addressed the scranton complaints elsewhere, (not to imply that they have or will be fully solved) but I do plan on fixing those slight lapses

Unfortunately, the meeting point between the mundane and the fantastical has always been a particularly delightful topic for me, so that bit is definitely a matter of taste

As for the ending, I mmaaaay make it a bit less ambiguous? I do like how soft, quiet, and foreboding it turned out though so if I do it will not be by much

Thank you for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, these are valid complaints-

But I do not think they quite -that- useful

For one, they will be completely unaffected by an object that does not exert any kind of an effect on them, which from some browsing I did specifically to check is quite a number more than you would think

For another thing, they are imprecise, do not tell you -that- you are dealing with, difficult to rear, and perish to a variety of natural phenomena as well

However!

A failure rate is an excellent idea that did not cross my mind that I will be implementing in future edits!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it's intended to be mundane so there's not much to be had for that.

I'm personally just delighted by the juxtaposition of the mundane and the supernatural, but that is definitely a matter of taste!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Scorpion451's analysis is not exactly right, and I suppose that, given how little attention the mechanism of their sensitivity is given in article some clarification is in order, and perhaps an edit. (If/when I do edit this, it will be when I fix the colors and the tone blips, as well)

The intention is not that they are sensitive to some underlying source of anomalies.

Rather, they are sensitive to any change in their environment, just as actual mayflies are sensitive to almost any tampering with their environment, regardless of origin. Along these lines, the article mentions several times that industrial chemicals and natural radiation and temperature changes will effect them just the same as foreign thoughts and warped spacetime

There is not just one thing they are sensitive to, that was never the concept; rather, as an indicator species, they are tied to the health of their environment, and sensitive to anything that is not part of it.

Ergo, they are not sensitive to something because it is anomalous.

They are sensitive to it because it affects them and the space they inhabit in a way that they are not prepared to compensate for, regardless of what it is.

On another note, your engineered concept is an interesting take on it and I may consider a rewrite along those lines some time in the future

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll say I wasn't confused by it for more than a second because 'imago' appears in the image caption.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I immediately thought of SRAs as well. Where this differs is that the Foundation recognizes it as anomalous. Sure, they can use it, but even if it's not suggested, there's nothing saying this is a reliable system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehh, this scip is nothing more than a tool for detecting other scips. Though I don't really have an issue with the idea that all anomalous phenomena can apparently be detected by a single entity, this piece does nothing more than straightforwardly lay out the concept. It develops fairly predictably and doesn't even attempt to explore any implications of using the animals. SCP-2000 is more interesting in that there is an attempt at exploring the mystery of the facility's existence, plus the whole idea is just depressing (and executed in such a way). This didn't elicit any emotional or intellectual response from me, frankly. The idea itself just seems so mundane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And just because things are radically different, doesn't mean they can't be deeply connected.

One fundamental force, many manifestations.

All of which happen to be things that the Foundation considers anomalous? I don't buy it. Is it possible, in-universe, that there's some force of nature that underlies all of these phenomena and makes this single species of mayfly die? Yeah, but I wouldn't bet my pocket lint on it, much less money.

Incidentally: I would be much more keen on a version where the mayflies were bio-engineered for the purpose (by someone other than the Foundation) and are sensitive to, like, half of these fields. If someone who does understand a few of these weird things deliberately set about to engineer a mayfly with a weakness to each of them individually, that would actually be plausible, and it renders the complaint about being "too useful" null. I would still have to judge it on its other merits, of course, but I think it would be a stronger piece as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I look at it like this:

Just because you know that something works, and that it can be used to observe something, doesn't mean you know why it works. And just because things are radically different, doesn't mean they can't be deeply connected.

One of the greatest breakthroughs of the early 1800s? The galvanometer, which allowed the precise measurement of these newly discovered "electrical" phenomena.

One of the greatest breakthroughs of the late 1800's? Figuring out why the hell galvanometers worked, why magnets screwed with them, and why they would sometimes measure currents when they weren't connected to anything: Magnetism and electricity were somehow related as a single "electromagnetism" force.

Breakthrough of the early 1900's? Why they did weird things around those "radio-active" minerals and those weird "cathode tube" things. Electromagnetism became electromagnetic radiation.

Breakthrough of the late 1900's? Finally figuring out that electromagnetic radiation is actually just one part of the electroweak force- which is also responsible for things like friction and the fact that your hand doesn't pass through your keyboard while you're typing. One fundamental force, many manifestations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel similarly. There's no sensible link between the types of fields the article mentions beyond "having a field" (which would also include anything with an electric or magnetic field, among other things) and "things found on things the Foundation wants to contain". It just reads like a convenient excuse to give the Foundation a tool to help contain/retrieve/monitor things, because that's also the only in-universe explanation that makes sense, and I'm not a fan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll agree with Kalinin re: "couple of minor lapses in tone" - "malevolent" was the one that really stuck out to me - but I love this idea completely. It's almost as if I'm some sort of biologist by training, huh?…

An indicator species for anomalies. I bloody love it. I do agree that Scranton anchors are a bit OP and deus ex - although it never really bothered me quite as much as it evidently did some of the above posters - but this, as Scorpion451 says, is juuuust inconvenient enough to use so as not to seem like plot-glue.

Sorry, my judgment is clouded by how much I love the idea. I can't give any specific, proper critique. Just take my rabid upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't perfect, but I did enjoy it. The addenda at the end are a little too vague.

My one big gripe is when you put 'elevated disturbance' in yellow text. That makes it impossible to read unless you highlight it. Just a little immersion-breaking.

Otherwise, good job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember seeing one of your drafts for this in the chat. Good job on improving this!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah. Glad to see you posted this.

I found this kind of AWS to be an interesting way of detecting whether an anomaly breaches containment. Its both outside the box and has enough limitations to be very interesting and provide a lot of potential tale fodder. I'm also happy to see the amount of blackboxes that were in the original drafts has been cut down.

+1. I really dig it, and the Site-64 nod.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I think I am beginning to prefer "imagoes", since "imagines" is confusingly spelled the same as the common verb "imagines" (aka a homograph). Still, I suggested it because I saw it used in the small amount of bug-science I did in college.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On #3, pretty much any way is you want to pluralize it is valid. It's one of those weird "eh, close enough" third declension words latin ripped off from an older language and stuck whatever endings sounded right on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it. Any sufficiently analyzed magic is indistinguishable from science.

This is the sort of anomaly detector I can get behind, too- inaccurate, non-portable, slow…but also an ideal bellwether for both big picture, long term observations and short term "you can panic now" alerts of anomalous activity…that still don't actually tell you anything about why you should panic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you are correct. fixed! will update the colors when i am at a computer again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. I was about to change "spacial", but I checked the dictionary first, and it turns out to be a real word. Still, you might want to change it to the more common "spatial".

2. The green-on-white text is really hard for me to read, and the yellow-on-white is even harder. I'd suggest using darker shades of green and yellow for better contrast, or else putting the color on the cell background rather than on the text.

3. Question: Isn't it "imagines"? Or do bug-scientists really use "imagos"? (Actually I don't know. I am not a bug-expert.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From a technical standpoint, it's not bad. There are a couple of minor lapses in tone here and there ("great care" and "just the last two decades" being somewhat colloquial and subjective descriptors in this context), but nothing that really breaks my immersion. The logs of the sites with populations of mayflies and their statuses struck me as right on the edge of providing too much filler for the sake of verisimilitude. It wasn't so much of a slog that I would necessarily fault that, but I can see how others might.

My main issue is that these are operating on essentially the same principle as Scranton anchors. In both things, reality has some sort of measurable, objective quality to it that can be used as a gauge and to inform predictable relationships. That's a concept that I reject because a) the idea that something like reality is somehow objectively measurable (and by extension, "the occult", "telepathic," or whatever) is completely ridiculous and b) it completely negates the sense of the uncanny and the inherently uncomprehendable that is one of the most important thematic elements of the site, at least to me.

By assigning clear values to what it means to be anomalous, to the point that we can now have convenient caches of indicator species that lets us know what's up, we've brought the anomalous back into the realm of science. For me as a reader, anytime that happens, I go from reading what is at its heart a magical realist narrative about the fantastic, to reading something boring and pedestrian. I know that other have different experiences, but to me, stuff like this saps interest and breaks immersion by introducing concepts that make absolutely no sense to me whatsoever.

I also don't care for the vagueness of the kicker at the end. With so little information about what the subject of the addenda are, you might as well have not even included them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, so this is well-written and interesting. I really do enjoy the canary-in-the-coalmine effect. Unfortunately, there's a downside.

The flies are basically sensitive to "SCP objects", which implies that either all anomalies have a common origin or mechanism or at least all alter reality using the extremely broad list of ways you provided. That's a controversial stance among the continuity.

You don't talk about failure rate or what percentage of known SCPs these things are or aren't sensitive to. It just makes them too damn convenient. Want to know if an object is anomalous? Stick it in a room with the bugs. Want to find every single uncontained SCP? Walk around with the bugs. It's the same problem people had with Kant Counters, but in a biological form even more powerful.1

No vote. I actually enjoy Scranton Reality Anchors and occasionally am down with Humes, but even to me this just seems too powerful.

Footnotes
1. Edit: if you weren't around for that, there was a pretty big controversy on the site after SCP-2000 introduced Scranton Realty Anchors, which prevent manipulation of reality. Someone then expanded that to the idea to create Humes as a measure of the stability of reality, and asserted that some SCPs mess with reality in a way measurable by Humes. People objected to this on two fronts: 1) having fictional anti-anomaly supertech gave the Foundation a trump card for every anomaly, which is boring, and 2) a lot of people don't canon a single origin or mechanism for all SCPs, which having a measure like Humes implies. In the end, people just kind of headcanoned the devices or not, but people will vote based on that preference.

item.title: Feels nice to have it up
item.content:encoded: Had a lot of help in the chat, particular thanks to Rimple and Conwell
